---
title: "A Community Reference Library for Computational Economics"
subtitle: "QuantEcon · CEF 2026 pre-conference · Venice · 28 June 2026"
author: "Matt McKay — QuantEcon"
date: "2026-06-28"
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme/quantecon.scss]
    logo: assets/qe-logo-large.png
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.06
    min-scale: 0.2
    max-scale: 2.0
    slide-number: c/t
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    progress: true
    hash: true
    center: true
    embed-resources: true
    include-after-body:
      text: |
        <script>
          (function () {
            var s = document.getElementById('title-slide');
            if (!s || s.querySelector('.title-url')) return;
            var a = document.createElement('a');
            a.className = 'title-url';
            a.href = 'https://quantecon.github.io/conference-cef2026/';
            a.target = '_blank';
            a.rel = 'noopener';
            a.setAttribute('data-preview-link', 'false');
            a.textContent = 'quantecon.github.io/conference-cef2026';
            s.appendChild(a);
          })();
        </script>
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#07365c"
---

## A dozen serious toolkits — no easy way to find them {.spread}

The field has a dozen mature toolkits, across **MATLAB, Python, and Julia**:

<div class="chips">
  <span class="chip mat">Dynare <span class="lang">MATLAB</span></span>
  <span class="chip mat">VFI Toolkit <span class="lang">MATLAB</span></span>
  <span class="chip mat">GDSGE <span class="lang">MATLAB</span></span>
  <span class="chip py">G-Cubed / SYM <span class="lang">Python / SYM</span></span>
  <span class="chip py">HARK / Econ-ARK <span class="lang">Python</span></span>
  <span class="chip py">GEModelTools <span class="lang">Python</span></span>
  <span class="chip py">sequence-jacobian <span class="lang">Python</span></span>
  <span class="chip py">Mesa <span class="lang">Python</span></span>
  <span class="chip py">Dolo / Dolark <span class="lang">Py · Jl</span></span>
  <span class="chip jl">BASEforHANK <span class="lang">Julia</span></span>
  <span class="chip cpp">Eurace / FLAME <span class="lang">C++</span></span>
  <span class="chip">AGENTBLOCKS</span>
</div>

Each has genuinely distinct strengths. But researchers have **no easy way to compare them**, **no shared library of canonical models**, and **no on-ramp to the ecosystem as a whole**.

<p class="pull center">Real diversity in how we solve models — and no easy way to see across it.</p>

::: {.notes}
Good afternoon — I'm Matt McKay from QuantEcon. In the next ten minutes I want to
put one concrete proposal on the table: a community-owned reference library that
documents the same canonical models across all of our toolkits.

So — a dozen serious toolkits, three languages. Each genuinely distinct. But
there's no single place to put them side by side. New users default to whatever
their advisor uses; experienced researchers stay locked into the first tool they
learned. We have plurality, but it's hard to actually *see* it or compare across
it.
:::

## A Community Reference Library {.spread}

<p class="lead">A single, community-owned library documenting the field's canonical models — **the same model in every toolkit**, side by side, open and runnable.</p>

```{=html}
<div class="cards three">
  <div class="card blue">
    <h3>Community-owned</h3>
    <p>Authored &amp; maintained by the projects themselves &mdash; each keeps credit and editorial control.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card teal">
    <h3>Cross-toolkit</h3>
    <p>The same canonical models across MATLAB, Python &amp; Julia &mdash; comparable, side by side.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card amber">
    <h3>Open &amp; executable</h3>
    <p>Every model runs &mdash; open-source and reproducible, published on Jupyter Book.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

<p class="pull center">The shared library — and the on-ramp — the field has been missing.</p>

::: {.notes}
So here's the answer to that fragmentation: one community-owned reference library
that documents the field's canonical models — the same model implemented in every
toolkit, side by side, open and runnable. Three things define it. It's
community-owned — authored and maintained by the projects themselves, who keep
credit and editorial control. It's cross-toolkit — the same canonical models
across MATLAB, Python and Julia, comparable in one place. And it's open
and executable — every model runs, published on Jupyter Book. It's the shared
library, and the on-ramp, the field has been missing. Now, how it's built.
:::

## The proposal — same model, multiple tools {.spread}

Each project authors its own work and contributes **two things**:

```{=html}
<div class="contribs">
  <div class="contrib base">
    <span class="num">1</span>
    <div class="ct"><strong>Shared baseline model</strong><span>coordinated across your group &mdash; directly comparable across toolkits</span></div>
  </div>
  <div class="contrib hero">
    <span class="num">2</span>
    <div class="ct"><strong>Hero tutorial</strong><span>your author's choice &mdash; the problem where your toolkit shines</span></div>
  </div>
</div>
```

<table class="qe-matrix">
<thead>
  <tr><th>Family</th><th>Shared baselines</th><th>Toolkits coordinated around them</th></tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
  <tr>
    <td class="cls">Heterogeneous-agent</td>
    <td class="base">Aiyagari &middot; 1-asset HANK</td>
    <td class="tools"><span class="chip mat">VFI Toolkit</span><span class="chip py">HARK</span><span class="chip py">Dolo</span><span class="chip py">GEModelTools</span><span class="chip py">SSJ</span><span class="chip mat">GDSGE</span><span class="chip jl">BASEforHANK</span><span class="chip mat">Dynare v7</span></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td class="cls">Representative-agent / DSGE</td>
    <td class="base">RBC &middot; 3-eq NK (RANK)</td>
    <td class="tools"><span class="chip mat">Dynare</span><span class="chip py">Dolo</span><span class="chip py">SSJ</span><span class="chip mat">GDSGE</span><span class="chip py">G-Cubed</span><span class="chip jl">Dynare.jl</span></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td class="cls">Agent-based</td>
    <td class="base">Boltzmann&ndash;Gibbs &middot; Lengnick ABM</td>
    <td class="tools"><span class="chip py">Mesa</span><span class="chip cpp">Eurace / FLAME</span><span class="chip">AGENTBLOCKS</span></td>
  </tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<p class="small muted">AGENTBLOCKS contributes reusable components, not a runnable model. Agent-based is the least-settled family &mdash; Boltzmann&ndash;Gibbs and Lengnick (2013) are a <strong>starting point</strong>; suggestions for a better baseline welcome.</p>

::: {.notes}
Here's the proposal, made concrete. A community-owned library — same model,
multiple tools, side by side. Crucially, each project authors and maintains its
own implementations. Three families where several toolkits genuinely overlap;
for each, two shared baselines — an introductory rung and an advanced rung — so
a group can grow from the simple case into the hard one. We coordinate the
projects in that row so a reader can open Aiyagari in VFI Toolkit next to
Aiyagari in HARK next to Aiyagari in SSJ — same notation, directly comparable —
and then step up to the 1-asset HANK baseline the same way. And each project
also contributes an author's-choice hero showcase for the problem where its
toolkit shines. Exact coverage is for the working group to set; this is the
shape of it.
:::

## What a "shared baseline" means {.spread}

```{=html}
<div class="bl-ladder-wrap">
  <div class="bl-ladder">
    <div class="bl-fam-head fam-dsge"><span class="fk">Representative agent</span>DSGE</div>
    <div class="bl-fam-head fam-het"><span class="fk">Heterogeneous agent</span>Incomplete markets</div>
    <div class="bl-fam-head fam-abm"><span class="fk">Agent-based</span>Bottom-up macro</div>

    <div class="bl-rung intro fam-dsge"><span class="rk">Introductory</span><h4>RBC growth</h4><p>Real business cycle &mdash; the DSGE starting point</p></div>
    <div class="bl-rung intro fam-het"><span class="rk">Introductory</span><h4>Aiyagari</h4><p>General equilibrium &mdash; wealth as an <em>equilibrium</em> object</p></div>
    <div class="bl-rung intro fam-abm"><span class="rk">Introductory</span><h4>Boltzmann&ndash;Gibbs exchange</h4><p>An <em>emergent</em> distribution &rarr; Gini</p></div>

    <div class="bl-thread wealth"><div class="seg"><span class="t-k">Inequality &rarr;</span><span class="cap">equilibrium vs emergent &mdash; one question, two mechanisms</span></div></div>

    <div class="bl-rung adv fam-dsge"><span class="rk">Advanced</span><h4>3-equation New Keynesian</h4><p>RANK &mdash; representative-agent closure</p><span class="tag">+ 25bp monetary shock</span></div>
    <div class="bl-rung adv fam-het"><span class="rk">Advanced</span><h4>1-asset HANK</h4><p>Heterogeneous-agent New Keynesian</p><span class="tag">+ 25bp monetary shock</span></div>
    <div class="bl-rung adv fam-abm"><span class="rk">Advanced</span><h4>Lengnick (2013)</h4><p>Baseline macro&ndash;ABM</p></div>

    <div class="bl-thread shock"><div class="seg"><span class="t-k">Same 25bp shock &rarr;</span><span class="cap">overlay the two IRFs &mdash; the signature <strong>RANK vs HANK</strong> comparison of the last decade</span></div></div>
  </div>
  <div class="spec-flow">
    <div class="spec-end in">
      <span class="se-k">Input</span>
      <span class="se-t">Shared Specification</span>
      <span class="se-sub">the agreed standard</span>
    </div>
    <div class="spec-arrow">&rarr;</div>
    <div class="spec-core">
      <span class="sc-k">What we decide next</span>
      <span class="sc-label">Decisions</span>
      <ol class="sc-list">
        <li><span class="n">1</span>Income process &amp; grids</li>
        <li><span class="n">2</span>Model closure</li>
        <li><span class="n">3</span>Calibration targets</li>
        <li><span class="n">4</span>Comparison contract</li>
      </ol>
    </div>
    <div class="spec-arrow">&rarr;</div>
    <div class="spec-end out">
      <span class="se-k">Output</span>
      <span class="se-t">Jupyter Notebooks</span>
      <span class="se-sub">with a model to solve</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.notes}
This is what "shared baseline" means in practice. Three families across the
columns, two rungs down each — an introductory model and an advanced one — so a
group can start simple and grow into the hard case. Representative-agent: RBC up
to a three-equation New Keynesian (RANK). Heterogeneous-agent: Aiyagari up to
one-asset HANK. Agent-based: a Boltzmann–Gibbs money-exchange model up to
Lengnick's baseline macro-ABM. The two coloured bands are the punchline. First:
the same 25-basis-point monetary shock runs in both advanced rungs — RANK and
HANK — so overlaying the two impulse responses gives the signature
RANK-versus-HANK comparison of the last decade. Second: inequality
links the two introductory rungs — in Aiyagari the distribution is an equilibrium
object, in the agent-based model it emerges bottom-up — one question, two
mechanisms. A word on comparability, because this is where it gets real: pinning
the income process and grids matters, but so does the model closure — the Taylor
rule, the shock, and especially the fiscal rule, since Ricardian equivalence
fails in HANK — and even then, solution-method differences move results. So the
contract differs by family: DSGE and HANK compare on impulse responses and steady
states, the heterogeneous-agent rungs add distributional moments, and agent-based
models compare on ensemble moments across seeds, not a single path. One thing to
flag if pressed: a one-asset HANK calibrated to aggregate wealth has low MPCs and
looks close to RANK, so we'd calibrate to a target MPC for the comparison to show
what it should. Rather than hand any of this down, we'd agree the spec with the
working group and distribute it as template Jupyter notebooks — defaults like
Rouwenhorst with seven states are a starting point, not a decree.
:::

## What the proposal aims for {.spread}

```{=html}
<div class="aims">
  <div class="card blue primary">
    <h3>A community asset</h3>
    <p>The field's first cross-toolkit reference — <strong>built by and for the community</strong>. A single entry point that lowers the search cost of a fragmented field, lets researchers compare toolkits side by side, and showcases each project through its hero tutorial — all on <strong>Jupyter Book</strong>, a modern code-first publishing platform.</p>
  </div>

  <p class="aim-label">Building towards</p>
  <div class="cards">
    <div class="card coral">
      <h3>Substrate for AI assistance</h3>
      <p>Exactly the corpus an AI research assistant needs:</p>
      <div class="chips">
        <span class="chip query">"Which tool fits my incomplete-markets model?"</span>
        <span class="chip query">"Translate my Dynare model to sequence-jacobian."</span>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="card teal">
      <h3>A proving ground for standards</h3>
      <p>As shared schemas emerge — steady states, IRFs, Jacobians — the library demonstrates them in working code, and becomes the testbed for <strong>Econ-ARK's Project Bellman</strong> semantic representation.</p>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.notes}
One priority, and two things it builds toward. The priority — first and
foremost — is a community asset: the field's first cross-toolkit reference,
built by and for the community. It's a single entry point that lowers the search
cost of a fragmented field — a dozen toolkits with no easy way to compare them; it
lets researchers put toolkits side by side on the same models; and it gives each
project a showcase through its hero tutorial. And it's all published on Jupyter
Book, the modern code-first platform we already run at scale. That's the
foundation, and it delivers value on its own. From that foundation, two things
follow. One — the part I'm most excited about — it becomes
the substrate for AI assistance. An assistant for this field is only as good as
the corpus behind it, and right now that corpus doesn't exist in structured,
cross-toolkit form. This library *is* that corpus: it lets an assistant answer
"which tool fits my incomplete-markets model?" or "translate my Dynare model to
sequence-jacobian" with grounded examples — and it's exactly what Econ-ARK's
semantic-representation work needs to validate against. And two, it becomes a
proving ground for the standards this working group will define, demonstrated in
working code, not just specified on paper. But the foundation comes first — the
library itself. QuantEcon brings the engineering capacity to build the glue —
which I'll come to.
:::

## QuantEcon Infrastructure

**A community partner** — already running open-source **executable book** infrastructure:

```{=html}
<div class="statband compact">
  <div class="stat"><span class="num">~31k</span><span class="lbl">monthly users</span></div>
  <div class="stat"><span class="num">172</span><span class="lbl">countries</span></div>
  <div class="stat"><span class="num">274</span><span class="lbl">lectures</span></div>
</div>
```

```{=html}
<div class="cards">
  <div class="card blue">
    <h3>Executable Jupyter Books</h3>
    <p><strong>Code-first publishing</strong> — prose, maths, and runnable code in one source that executes and renders straight to a website. MyST / JupyterBook 2, the stack behind our lecture sites, with a book theme we maintain.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card teal">
    <h3>Multi-language execution &amp; CI</h3>
    <p>Python &amp; Julia in Jupyter — with or without GPU instances for compute-intensive workflows. Interactive tutorials via Binder or Colab.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card amber">
    <h3>MATLAB support</h3>
    <p>Run locally via the MATLAB kernel for Jupyter — MATLAB needs a licence, so we publish pre-rendered output. Home for Dynare, VFI Toolkit, GDSGE.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card coral">
    <h3>The ecosystem glue</h3>
    <p>A GitHub-based auto-publishing platform, hosting for community pages, and help with continuous integration and testing.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.notes}
We already run exactly the infrastructure this needs. Our lecture sites are
executable Jupyter Books — around 31,000 readers a month across 172 countries —
built on MyST and JupyterBook 2 with a book theme we maintain. Python and Julia
execute in CI, with GPU instances when a workflow needs them, and tutorials run
interactively in Binder or Colab — both now support Julia as well as Python.
MATLAB is the hard case: it needs a licence, so it runs locally through the MATLAB
kernel for Jupyter and we publish pre-rendered output — that's the home for
Dynare, VFI Toolkit and GDSGE. And the glue: a GitHub-based auto-publishing
platform, hosting for community pages, and help with CI and testing. This is the
stack the library needs, and we already operate it.
:::

## A two-phase roadmap {.spread}

```{=html}
<div class="phases">
  <div class="phase one">
    <span class="ph-tag">Phase 1</span><span class="when">end of 2026</span>
    <h3>Community library</h3>
    <p>A static <strong>Jupyter Book</strong> site publishing tutorials on:</p>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>2 &times; baseline models</strong> &mdash; for cross-comparison of toolkits</li>
      <li><strong>1 &times; hero tutorial</strong> &mdash; per package / toolkit</li>
      <li>CI runs to check execution and report execution issues</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="phase two">
    <span class="ph-tag">Phase 2</span><span class="when">2027+</span>
    <h3>Reference standard</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>A library to test <strong>shared schemas</strong></li>
      <li>Cross-toolkit validation checks</li>
      <li>Semantic examples from Econ-ARK <strong>Project Bellman</strong> &mdash; semantics &amp; schema</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.notes}
Two phases. Phase one, through the end of 2026, is the community library itself — a
static Jupyter Book site publishing tutorials: two baseline models per group for
cross-toolkit comparison, one hero tutorial per package, and CI that runs every
model to check execution and report issues. Phase two, from 2027 onward,
turns it into a reference standard: a library that tests shared schemas,
cross-toolkit validation checks, and semantic examples from Econ-ARK's Project
Bellman — its semantics and schema work. Phase one delivers value on its own;
phase two is where the standards harden.
:::

## The ask — and who does what {.spread}

<p class="kicker">Register to participate today — plus, for Phase 1 (by end of 2026):</p>

- **Shared baseline models** — introductory and advanced, coordinated across your group — and a **hero tutorial**, your author's choice: the problem where your toolkit shines
- **Authored and maintained by your project** — you keep credit, editorial control, and the right to update or withdraw

```{=html}
<div class="roles two">
  <div class="role p">
    <h3>Projects</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>Author &amp; own <strong>MyST</strong> content <span class="muted">(Markdown or another format if preferred)</span></li>
      <li>Keep credit &amp; editorial control</li>
      <li>Collaborate via GitHub</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="role q">
    <h3>QuantEcon</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>Hosts, publishes &amp; builds the glue</li>
      <li>Develops supporting infrastructure (e.g. MATLAB)</li>
      <li>A shared home that champions every project</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>
```

<p class="small muted center">Governance sits with the SCE working group launched at this meeting.</p>

::: {.notes}
So here's the concrete ask. First, register to participate — today. Then, by the
end of 2026, two things from each project: shared baseline models — introductory
and advanced — that we coordinate across your group, and one author's-choice hero
tutorial for what your tool does best, both authored and maintained by you. And
to be clear about the division of labour: you author in MyST and collaborate
through GitHub, owning your content and keeping editorial control; QuantEcon is
the shared home that hosts, publishes, builds the glue, and develops the
supporting infrastructure — including the MATLAB path. This is a
high-visibility, high-impact ask.
:::

## Same model. Multiple tools. Side by side. {background-color="#07365c" .dark-slide}

<p class="lead">The field's first cross-toolkit reference — built by the community that owns these tools.</p>

- [**Register to participate**](https://github.com/QuantEcon/conference-cef2026/issues/new/choose) — today
- **Shared baselines + a showcase** from your toolkit — by end of 2026

<p style="margin-top:1em">[**Register your project →**](https://github.com/QuantEcon/conference-cef2026/issues/new/choose) github.com/QuantEcon/conference-cef2026 · Let's talk after the session.</p>

::: {.notes}
To close. This is the field's first cross-toolkit reference, and it only works
if it's built by the community that owns these tools — that's all of you. Two
things from me today: register to participate, and a shared baseline plus a
showcase from your toolkit by the end of the year. Registration is open now at
github.com/QuantEcon/conference-cef2026. I'd love to talk specifics with any of
you after the session. Thank you.
:::

## Support slides {background-color="#07365c" .dark-slide .center}

<p class="lead center">Reference material — for questions and discussion.</p>

::: {.notes}
Backup from here. I'll jump to these if a question calls for it.
:::

## Who is QuantEcon?

A non-profit building **open-source tools for computational economics** — used across research, central banks, and universities worldwide.

```{=html}
<div class="statband">
  <div class="stat"><span class="num">~31k</span><span class="lbl">monthly users</span></div>
  <div class="stat"><span class="num">172</span><span class="lbl">countries</span></div>
  <div class="stat"><span class="num">8</span><span class="lbl">lecture series</span></div>
  <div class="stat"><span class="num">30+</span><span class="lbl">workshops delivered</span></div>
</div>
```

```{=html}
<div class="cards">
  <div class="card blue">
    <h3>Lectures</h3>
    <p>Open-source curriculum from intro to advanced &mdash; Python, Julia &amp; JAX. Free to read, run, and adapt.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card teal">
    <h3>Books</h3>
    <p>Peer-reviewed textbooks &mdash; <em>Dynamic Programming</em>, <em>Economic Networks</em>, <em>Quantitative Macro&nbsp;Finance</em>.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card amber highlight">
    <h3>Infrastructure</h3>
    <p>The executable-book stack &mdash; MyST &amp; JupyterBook 2, plus the QuantEcon.py / .jl libraries. Founding member of the Executable Books Project.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="card coral">
    <h3>Training</h3>
    <p>Short courses delivered at the IMF, central banks, and universities worldwide.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

<p class="small muted center">Reach: quantecon.org analytics dashboard &mdash; monthly active users averaged over Dec 2025&ndash;May 2026, across 172 countries.</p>

::: {.notes}
A backup on who's making this, if it comes up. QuantEcon is a non-profit building
open-source tools for computational economics. Four pillars: our lecture series —
read by around thirty-one thousand people a month across a hundred and seventy-two
countries; our peer-reviewed textbooks; the executable-book infrastructure we build
and maintain, as a founding member of the Executable Books Project; and the training
we deliver at the IMF, central banks, and universities. Those reach numbers come
straight from our public analytics dashboard. The point: we already serve this whole
community at scale — which is exactly why we can host a shared reference library for it.
:::
